senses.gloss: the state of being awake, not being asleep; awakeness

examples.text: مَن اِدَّعَى أَنَّهُ رَأَى النَبِيَّ صَلَّى اللّٰهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَمَ فِي ٱليَقَظَةِ بَعْدَ وَفَاةِ النَبِيِ فَهْوَ كَاذِب
examples.english: Whoever claims to have seen the Prophet after his death while awake is a liar.

examples.text: الشَّعْبُ كَانَ يَقِظًا فِي مَطَالِبِهِ وَأَهْدَافِهِ.
examples.english: The people were alert in their demands and goals.

etymology.text: Verbal noun of يَقُظَ (yaquẓa, “to be awake”) (cf. اِسْتَيْقَظَ (istayqaẓa, “to wake up (oneself)”) and أَيْقَظَ (ʔayqaẓa, “to wake up (someone else)”), from the root ي ق ظ (y q ẓ), related to awakeness.

body.text: , occurring in the saying of the Et-Tihámee,

AlEayo$u nawomN waAlomaniy~apu yaqoZapN
                      
                     
                        waAlomaro'u bayonahumaA xayaAlN saAriY
Life is a state of sleep, and death is a state of waking; and man between the two is a night journeying phantom
]; but most hold it to be used only by poetic license. (TA.)
